summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – What, if any, time the children should spend with the father – Allegations of family violence – Where the Court finds the father has perpetrated family violence upon the mother in the presence, at times, of the children – Where the father has not demonstrated any real insight into his actions and behaviour – final Orders made for the children to spend time with the father as agreed and for the mother to have sole decision making responsibility

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BAUMANN J:

INTRODUCTION

These proceedings (of which only parenting issues now remain on foot) relate to a young couple, being the Applicant, Ms Saros born in 1986 (hereinafter referred to as “the mother”) and the Respondent, Mr Rattan born in 1986 (hereinafter referred to as “the father”). There are two children subject to these proceedings – X born in 2018 and Y born in 2022 (collectively “the children”). Having resolved all matters in relation to property – which I briefly detail later in these Reasons – the outstanding matters for determination centre around what time X and Y are to spend with their father.
The Orders which appear at the commencement of these Reasons are in the best interests of X and Y, at this time in their lives.

STATUTORY PATHWAY

Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) sets out the statutory provisions and considerations to be made when making a parenting order, with s 65A granting the Court power to make a parenting order as it thinks proper. Section 60B of the Act provides two underpinning objects – being that the best interests of the children are met, including by ensuring their safety (s 60B(a)) and effect must be given to the Convention on the Rights of the Child. During my term as a judicial officer, I have navigated the changing landscape for parenting orders from s 68F through s 60CC of the Act; the additional and primary considerations, and now a new regime contained within four sections of a new s 60CC of the Act. The destination, however, has never altered. It is what is in the best interests of the children, and as provided for by s 60CA of the Act, this must be regarded as the Court’s paramount consideration when making a parenting order.
In making orders that are in the children’s best interests, s 60CC(2) provides factors which the Court must take into consideration, being:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

As family violence is a relevant issue in these proceedings, I also must consider any history of family violence involving the children and any person caring for the child (s 60CC(2A)(a)) and any family violence order that may apply to the child or a member of the child’s family (s 60CC(2A)(b)). While a more comprehensive analysis in a narrative form will be discussed later in these Reasons, s 4AB of the Act provides a comprehensive definition of family violence. I am satisfied that I have considered the prescribed obligations of the Court when determining the best interests of X and Y.
Further, in circumstances where the final hearing in these proceedings has occurred long after 6 May 2024, the presumption of equal shared responsibility does not apply to this parenting case. While s 61C states that each parent has parental responsibility for a child, s 61D provides that the Court may allocate the responsibility for decision making in relation to major long-term decisions either jointly or solely.

BRIEF CONTEXTUAL HISTORY

The parties, who are both trained as educators, commenced their cohabitation in approximately July 2014 – having met in 2011 and commenced their relationship in 2013 when the parties were both their late twenties. Both parties worked at O School during this period, until late 2018.
The parties purchased their first home – a unit in Suburb S in December 2016 and were blessed with the birth of their first child, X, in 2018.
Following X’s birth, the mother continued to work O School (having utilised her maternity leave entitlements) three days per week. Similarly, the father left his position at O School to take on a role at another school, however ultimately left the profession to commence work as a marketing manager at an allied health service provider organisation. The father has remained working within the allied health and National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) industries, albeit self-employed from mid-2020, running his own business – P Company.
In March 2020, the parties received their first Intervention Order (“the 2020 Intervention Order”) as a result of police attendance following an altercation between the parties. The 2020 Intervention Order named the mother as the protected person; the father as the Respondent and expired in March 2021. The circumstances of this Order being made are discussed further in these Reasons.
For context, I also note that in March 2020, the Covid-19 pandemic dramatically changed the Australian societal landscape, with Australians nationally being subject to strict lockdowns. Unfortunately for these parties, Victoria was also subject to further lockdowns spanning over several months with all mandates being removed by approximately September 2022.
In January 2021, the parties purchased their former matrimonial home at Q Street, Suburb R, and sold their Suburb S unit shortly after in March 2021.
In 2022, the parties welcomed the birth of their daughter, Y.
The parties ultimately separated during second half of 2023 following an altercation between the parties, which I discuss further in these Reasons. At the time of separation, X was five years of age and Y was approximately 1 year old.
In or around this time of 2023, the mother obtained an interim Intervention Order, with herself and the children named as protected persons and the father named as the Respondent.

PROCEDURAL HISTORY

Proceedings in Division 2 of this Court were commenced after the mother obtained the interim Intervention Order, with the mother filing her Initiating Application on 31 August 2023, relating to parenting matters only. From the commencement of these proceedings, the mother has contended for orders that she have sole decision-making responsibility (parental responsibility as it then was).
The father, in his Response filed 19 October 2023, sought equal shared parental responsibility and that the children live with the parents on a week about basis.
Following an Order of a Judicial Registrar made 10 October 2023, Ms J of Legal Aid Victoria came on record as the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) on 13 November 2023. Shortly thereafter on 16 October 2023, an Order was made in chambers by consent by a Judicial Registrar for the appointment of Mr H as single expert for the purpose of preparing a family report. The matter proceeded to its first interim hearing before a Senior Judicial Registrar on 5 December 2023, which resulted in interim consent Orders being made for the children to live with the mother and spend supervised time with the father.
Following the production of the family report dated 27 November 2023 (filed 4 December 2023), a Senior Judicial Registrar (as they were at that time) made Orders on 27 February 2024 and delivered ex tempore Reasons for Judgment for the children to spend supervised time with the father:
(a) from 3.30pm to 6.30pm on Wednesday and Saturday in week one; and

(b) from 3.30pm to 6.30pm on Wednesday in week two.

On that occasion, the parents were also ordered to communicate via Our Family Wizard and that Dr G be appointed as single expert to prepare a psychiatric assessment of each of the parents (see Saros & Rattan [2024] FedCFamC2F 341).
On 15 March 2024, the mother amended her Initiating Application to introduce property proceedings, and on 9 May 2025 an Application in a Proceeding was filed seeking the children’s time with the father be suspended until the father undertook a hair follicle test (along with some procedural orders to progress the property proceedings).
On 21 June 2024, a Senior Judicial Registrar suspended the children’s time with the father pending further hearing and ordered both parents undertake hair follicle testing. Following the father filing a Response to the Application in a Proceeding, Orders were made for the children to remain living with their mother – conditional upon her attendance upon her treating psychologist, and the suspension of the children’s time with the father continued. The Order also contained a suite of property orders in relation to disclosure, valuation and real property (see Saros & Rattan (No 2) [2024] FedCFamC2F 780).
On 25 July 2024, a Division 2 Judge transferred the proceedings to Division 1 and listed the matter for a final hearing before the Honourable Justice Williams commencing on 7 April 2025 and made trial directions accordingly. Not long after being set down for final hearing, an Application in a Proceeding was filed by the mother’s own father seeking to be joined to the proceedings as a Respondent, in circumstances where a loan agreement between himself (and his wife) and the parties existed. That Application in a Proceeding was listed before me for Case Management Hearing on 12 November 2024, being the first time the matter came before me. At that hearing, the father appeared by video on his own behalf. Noting s 102NA applied to the proceedings on a mandatory basis, I discussed with the father his ability to gain representation under the Commonwealth Family Violence and Cross-Examination of Parties Scheme. Further, on this occasion:
(a) the mother’s parents, Mr C Saros and Mrs B Saros, were joined to the proceedings as the Second Respondents;

(b) a request for further information was issued to the Department of Families, Fairness and Housing pursuant to s 67ZBD of the Act;

(c) the scheduled final hearing commencing on 7 April 2025 was reallocated to me; and

(d) updated trial directions issued accordingly due to the Second Respondent’s involvement.

The final hearing commenced as scheduled on 7 April 2025, in Melbourne, with Ms Metherell of Counsel appearing on behalf of the mother, Mr Dudderidge of Counsel appearing on behalf of the father, Mr Hoult of Counsel appearing on behalf of the Second Respondents (the mother’s parents) and Mr James of Counsel appearing on behalf of the ICL. For completeness, I note the Orders made by consent on both 7 April 2025 and 8 April 2025 resolved all property and financial proceedings between both the husband and wife and the Second Respondents.

COMPETING PROPOSALS

ICL

Upon the closing of the evidence, the ICL handed up a proposed minute of orders sought, of which was marked as Exhibit 16 and is Appendix A to these Reasons. In her case outline filed 24 April 2025, the ICL sought to rely on a number of affidavits of the parties and the various single expert reports. On the first day of the hearing, Mr James of Counsel appearing on behalf of the ICL confirmed this was an error and that only the affidavits of Dr G (a single expert psychiatrist) and Mr H (the family report writer) would be relied upon in the ICL’s case.

The mother

In her case outline filed 2 April 2025, the mother sought minimal parenting orders, namely:
(a) the mother have sole responsibility for major long-term decisions, albeit informing the father of any serious health issue concerning the children;

(b) the children live with the mother;

(c) the children spend no time nor communicate with the father (with the exception that the father be permitted to send cards and gifts); and

(d) the mother be permitted to travel with the children outside of Australia.

In support of the orders she seeks, the mother relied upon various witnesses, including Family Contact Services Supervisor, Ms K; a family friend and previous lay supervisor, Ms L (who was subject to cross-examination) and her psychologist Ms M. Neither Ms M or Ms K were required for cross-examination.

The father

Despite being directed to do so, the father did not file a case outline. On the first day of the hearing, Mr Dudderidge of Counsel appearing on behalf of the father outlined the terms of the orders the father now sought. It was not until the following day that my chambers received a proposed minute sought on behalf of the father, which was subsequently marked Exhibit 1. Those orders ultimately sought:
(a) the children live with the mother;

(b) the children spend time with the father in a particularised graduated time regime – structured around the father completing multiple hair follicle drug testing (and not producing a positive result of illicit drug use) over the course of approximately 19 weeks initially supervised, following which another three-step transition period of six months would occur for the children to spend increased time with the father;

(c) following all graduated time and transition time periods, the children spend unsupervised time with the father every second Wednesday evening and each alternate week from after school on Friday until before school on Wednesday;

(d) the father be restrained from consuming alcohol, illicit substances or misusing prescription medicine whilst the children are in his care;

(e) the mother have sole decision-making responsibility for all major long-term decisions until such point the father progresses through all transition stages as discussed above, whereby decision-making responsibility would then be shared by the parents; and

(f) various ancillary orders in relation to travel, the children’s residence, education and medical matters.

ISSUES TO BE DETERMINED

Flowing from the final competing proposals articulated above, the Court is required to determine:
(a) who exercises decision making for major long term decisions;

(b) what time the father spends with the children, if any; and

(c) where time between the children and the father is ordered, what conditions, if any, should apply, including:

(i) supervision;

(ii) psychological support; and/or

(iii) alcohol and substance testing.

Prior to assessing the competing proposals within the matrix of the relevant considerations as set out earlier, I believe it is appropriate to deal with some discrete issues.

CREDIBLITY OF THE PARENTS

Whilst I deal with the evidence offered to the Court in respect of family violence shortly, in final submissions the ICL suggested that the mother’s evidence should be preferred, but that a “blanket” finding of credibility is not required, submitting a more nuanced approach needs to be adopted. Counsel for the mother adopted the ICL’s contention on credibility, whilst Counsel for the father elected to make no general submission about credibility at all.
As I soon explore when dealing with the seminal issue of family violence, overall, I found the father’s constant denial and attempts to reframe the facts to justify his either lack of recall or to provide a context for his behaviour, as leading me to form a strong conclusion that the father was a very unreliable historian. I am mindful, of course, of the opinions expressed by Dr G about the father, which I deal with below. However, I nonetheless found the father evasive in many of his responses; he sought to intellectualise the issue of family violence and at times was overly semantic. At times, he rudely interrupted or cut off Counsel for the mother, exhibiting, in my assessment, limited patience, and tried too hard to anticipate from the question alone, what answer Counsel was seeking to adduce.
It is to be remembered, and it fits with my experience as a trial judge of over 25 years, that most witnesses (particularly those parents in a parenting case) try to convey calmness, reasonableness and respect for the process. Sadly, the father did not demonstrate this demeanour consistently. I do not accept that his recent diagnosis of ADD explains his testimony as I observed it.
The father criticised the mother – in effect asserting she was exaggerating family violence events and otherwise seeking to engage in a narrative to portray herself as a victim of consistent family violence and coercive control by the father. I deal more completely with some of the family violence events next.
At times the mother’s evidence bordered on slight embellishment, but overall, I assess her as authentic and she tried to recall events honestly and frankly. The fact she was able to concede that her behaviour at times was less than appropriate (for example spitting on the father; hitting him), was a reflection of honesty rarely seen in the father’s evidence under cross-examination.

FAMILY VIOLENCE

As mentioned earlier, at the time of the hearing, the definition of family violence in s 4AB was as follows:
(1) For the purposes of this Act, family violence means violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member), or causes the family member to be fearful.
...
(3) For the purposes of this Act, a child is exposed to family violence if the child sees or hears family violence or otherwise experiences the effects of family violence.
In a very comprehensive trial affidavit, the mother, under the heading “family violence history”, set out a history which she says began in 2014 (see paragraphs 39 to 95).
Counsel for the ICL put to the father at the commencement of his cross-examination (and after Counsel for the mother had thoroughly put her client’s case to the father) that the mother asserted 38 incidents since 2014. The father, after initially denying any physical violence towards the mother or to X, later said that whilst denying most of the allegations, the context he gives is highly relevant.
Ms Metherell systematically put a number of incidents to the father, and without suggesting the answers given are totality of the father’s responses, the following examples make the point:
(a) In 2017, when the mother was pregnant with X, she says the father grabbed her “around the neck” and pushed her (paragraph 41). The father said the incident never happened. The father denied an incident in 2022/23 when during a verbal argument the mother says the father “grabbed me by the throat” (paragraph 83);

(b) When pressed about the mother’s allegation of choking, the father volunteered that sometime in 2017, he can recall when he grabbed the mother’s wrist as he was “concerned” she might hurt herself as she was holding a knife. No such allegation by the father was specifically put to the mother in her cross-examination;

(c) The father agreed that during an argument he tipped a tall chest of drawers onto a bed where the mother was lying (paragraph 43), but his excuse for doing so was unconvincing;

(d) The father denied that in 2019 he “slammed down” a plastic box near the mother as she was breastfeeding (paragraph 45), saying he “placed” the box down “somewhat recklessly but not aggressively”;

(e) The incident described by the mother at paragraph 50 relating to the father throwing a takeaway coffee cup and then “broke the aluminium mop handle over a chair” where the mother was sitting cuddling X, elicited an oral response that he only “bent” the handle. This incident caused the mother to make a complaint to police and resulted in separation for a short time after that. The police obtained an interim intervention order in 2020;

(f) Somewhat curiously, despite the interim intervention order being in place, the parties reconciled and lived together again. The mother, at paragraph 55, after alleging the father breached the intervention order (remembering they were still living together), explained why she “was too afraid to report those breaches to police”, by saying the father “threatened that if I called the Police he would lose his job and then the mortgage would not be paid and we would lose our home.” The father admitted he told the mother the “consequences” if she made these false allegations to police – namely losing the house;

(g) The child X started to “report” things to his care providers, including the father punching the wall. The father asks the Court to accept that the mother essentially “coached” the child to make the comment – a suggestion I reject. The “stories” X told his care givers at times seemed to me to be concerns expressed by a little boy who was witnessing some dysregulated behaviour by the father as his parents’ marriage was falling apart. Of particular concern is the father’s response to an allegation by X that his father hurt him. The father says that what really happened was that he was “restraining” the child when the child was uncontrollable and trying to kick the mother, who had only just given birth to Y. He said his response was not disproportionate and he “released” the child when he said he was uncomfortable – and well before the child’s breathing was impacted. The father seemed to have no insight into how this event shaped X’s concerns about the father. The incident caused the caregivers to mandatorily report to the Department, and gives context to the meeting that occurred at the pre-school in 2022 – the minutes speaking for themselves (Exhibit 9) (paragraph 65);

(h) Paragraphs 67 to 69 relate to the “balustrade” incident in February 2023 when the mother says the child Y suffered a minor cut. X witnessed the incident when the father “punched” the timber when “frustrated” and was arguing with the mother over a vacuum cleaner. He denied the mother’s evidence that in light of the earlier Department investigation, he was concerned that the parents had their “stories straight” when they took Y to the hospital. The mother did not give any explanation to the hospital about how the incident occurred. I accept the mother’s version, and note in cross-examination the father said it was a “reasonably foreseeable” accident which was not deliberate and that he felt terrible about it;

(i) The tensions in the relationship were clearly increasing, and although the father says there were many loud arguments, and at times X was distressed and even sought to intervene, the father asks the Court to accept he was “often the person to walk away”. The incident on 18 April 2023, after X (then aged five years) told his father to leave, was the time the mother says, and I accept this evidence, the father said words to the effect, directed to X, “how would you feel if you didn’t have a dad anymore. Maybe I’ll go kill myself and it will be all your fault.” The father denies he used these words. I believe the mother, who further observed that X was “inconsolable” – as he would have been, I am sure;

(j) Text exchanges that occurred on 20 April 2023 (marked Exhibit 10) reveal the total inability of the parties to communicate effectively. It is true that the Department received five s 38 reports in relation to X and Y between July 2023 and September 2024, with an earlier report on 27 September 2022 progressing to the investigation and assessment phase (see Exhibit 11); and

(k) The father says he purchased an “axe” for the mother as a present for Christmas 2023 (post separation). The mother says it distressed her. The father sought to explain his choice of the axe as something beneficial for the mother. This version by the father was wholly unconvincing. I accept the mother’s evidence that she saw the “gift” as a reminder to her of the father’s past abusive behaviour.

There are more incidents than those recorded in these Reasons. However, I have reached a conclusion that the father has perpetrated family violence upon the mother in the presence, at times, of the children. I am less satisfied he has intentionally physically abused X or Y, but they have suffered emotional harm.
In making this finding, I do not ignore or excuse the occasional inappropriate behaviour of the mother, which she was able to admit to and express true regret.
The father continued to minimise his actions and his contribution to the incidents – over 10 years. Such a resistance, as I saw demonstrated, raises concerns about not only the father’s insight into his behaviour and its effect on the mother and the children, but without any real insight, how he might begin a process of change, that is sustainable.
In this respect, the father’s evidence at paragraph 29 of his trial affidavit, on the evidence I have seen, is not supportable, namely:
[Ms Saros] tends to portray herself either as a victim of injustice or as a [s]aviour standing against it. In doing so, she often recasts events in a different light to support this narrative. This behaviour becomes particularly evident when she feels she is losing control of a situation, especially regarding [X] and [Y].
The father points to some inconsistencies by the mother in her recalling events (for example the event at separation on 15 July 2023) as told to police; the fact she did not report incidents to authorities or her parents and, I infer from the tenor of the mother’s cross-examination, that she remained in the relationship when she was continually abused (she says) not only with X but then, after conceiving Y, remained for two years after her birth.
I do not accept the father’s theoretical assumption about the mother – and his assertion this behaviour shows she really does not fear him as she asserts. Rather, my assessment was, where the mother was capable of articulating generously the father loves the children and can offer some beneficial traits and interests to the children, the father’s inability to alter his impulsive and coercive behaviour just got to the stage she could not put up with it anymore and that she feels genuinely that the benefits of having the children spend time with the father are outweighed by the risks he poses.
I rely upon these findings as I try to illuminate the pathway to my ultimate difficult decision. I now make some assessments of the expert evidence.

DR G – CONSULTANT PSYCHIATRIST

Dr G was appointed a single expert to provide an assessment of the parents, which he did individually and in separate consultations. He has briefly cross-examined.

The father

On 28 May 2024, Dr G undertook a psychiatric assessment of the father for two hours, culminating in a report dated 28 May 2024, which is before the Court.
Dr G was not challenged on the foundational facts contained in his report and his reliance on material provided by the ICL. He was aware the father had engaged psychologist Ms N over 13 sessions since July 2023, who he noted had “reported improvement in the father’s impulse-control including his level of anger”. He accepted the father had been diagnosed with ADD and had been prescribed Ritalin.
Dr G’s report on the father opined, inter alia:
(a) the father was “noticeably sensitive to any possible signs of questions, comments or even language that might indicate that he would be thought of as aggressive or destructive” (paragraph 59);

(b) the father is “not fully aware of the extent of his anger and dysregulative behaviour, nor their impact upon the mother or the children”. He needs to seek professional help for this “impairment in insight” (paragraph 60);

(c) whilst it is important to note the father suffers from “an Adjustment Disorder secondary to the separation from the mother, and diminished contact with the children”, the father appears “to be very vulnerable to loss” and his “behaviour coming to the child care centre in an attempt to take [Y]” (the father says for lunch) “can be seen as a maladaptive action to retain control of the situation by keeping a child closer to him” (paragraph 61);

(d) noting, in effect, it is a matter for the Court to determine which competing versions of family violence is correct “considering the intensity and duration of [the father’s] dysregulation, [the father] has not taken the need for treatment seriously” and his symptoms are only “partially treated” (paragraph 64); and

(e) as the Court broadly believes the mother’s allegations of violence and some threats the father has made (nor the evidence of Ms L), a recommendation for ongoing treatment was made (at paragraph 65), to help the father “develop more adaptive strategies to cope with his dysregulative episodes, and to be able to learn how to tolerate signs of rejection and deal with loss. These aims may take a few years to bear fruit” (emphasis added).

Under cross-examination, Dr G confirmed he was concerned the father was not engaging with a therapist (the father having admitted that the last time he saw Ms N was in December 2024) on a weekly basis and his sense of concern about the father remained, and that his views had not altered. He opined if the Court ordered “no time” between the children and the father, the father would carry grievances, and as he genuinely loves the children, he will likely blame others.

The mother

On 22 May 2024, Dr G undertook a psychiatric assessment of the mother for two hours, culminating in a report dated 22 May 2024, which is before the Court.
Dr G was not challenged on the foundational facts contained in his report and his reliance on material provided by the ICL. Dr G was aware the mother has seen psychologist, Ms M on almost a fortnightly basis since December 2023, having previously seen a family violence counsellor from August 2023 to March 2024, chiefly for practical advice.
Dr G’s report of the mother opined, inter alia, that:
(a) at paragraph 62, with the mother disclosing a lengthy and detailed history of aggressive behaviour of the father, including physical assaults, dysregulated angry outburst and damage to property, “if her account of the father’s behaviour in the past is to be believed by the Court, then [the mother’s] fears would be a justifiable response”;

(b) the mother’s affect in the assessment “would be consistent with an exposure to trauma”, and she “conveyed a sense of being terrified for her life” (paragraph 63);

(c) the mother:

...describes minimising the level of seriousness of the escalating behaviour, often either creating excuses for the father’s actions, believing that it would somehow improve or doubting the evidence of her own perceptions when confronted by the father’s complete denials...and as a result, allowed the behaviour to continue largely unchecked.
(paragraph 65)

(d) the mother “admitted that she had been physical towards X at times, when he had been oppositional and defiant. However, she denied this had been excessive...”. Dr G opined that the mother’s “frustration can be understood as a maladaptive response to a situation where she may not be able to quickly find a better approach to the problem” (paragraph 67); and

(e) if the Court were to believe the mother’s account of the family violence history “then her decision to withhold the children would have been an understandable response to ensure their safety”. Her sessions with her psychologist Ms M, which should continue “are helpful in providing emotional support, but would not be expected to result in a reduction in anxiety without a concomitant increase in a sense of safety for herself and the children” (paragraph 68).

Under cross-examination, Dr G did not have any new data that would cause him to change his opinions. If the Court made orders which were for the father to spend time with the children, the mother’s anxiety might get worse, he said.

MR H

Mr H, an experienced clinical psychologist, was appointed jointly to prepare a family report. Mr H conducted interviews on 19 October 2023, culminating a report dated 27 November 2023, which is before me. The report was prepared before the psychiatric assessments by Dr G, which I have just discussed. However, despite not being asked to prepare an updated report, Mr H confirmed (before his cross-examination), that he had read the reports of the contact supervisor Ms K; the two assessments by Dr G and the parties’ trial affidavits.
Returning to his report, some of the most relevant opinions and observations can be summarised as follows:
(a) The father presented as a “tense and anxious man” who said that his recent diagnosis of “ADD and rejection Sensitivity Dysphoria... explained much about his personal difficulties” (paragraph 11);

(b) Noting (at the time of interviews) the father had not spent time with the children since August 2023, the father expressed confusion and “denied allegations of family violence, and he questioned the motivation of [the mother] in denying him access to the children” (paragraph 14);

(c) Whilst admitting “that there were times that he lost his temper” he “denied that he ever lost control to the point that he either hurt or assaulted either [the mother] or the children”. The father (at paragraph 16) is recorded as raising many complaints about the mother’s behaviour physically hitting X; forcing the child under a cold shower and that the mother’s “over reaction was common”;

(d) After musing over the father’s recollections and differences with the mother’s version (see paragraphs 17 to 26), the father’s statements reflect both his denial and his belief of the mother’s exaggerating, embellishing or simply that the mother is seeking to portray him in a negative light;

(e) The father’s interaction with the children, observed by Mr H, was described as progressing “unremarkably well” with X obviously enjoying his father’s attention (paragraph 26);

(f) The mother presented as at times emotional as she recounted the circumstances and events that culminated in the decision to separate from the father. She initially agreed to the children having time with their father “supervised by the paternal grandfather and his wife on three occasions” however the mother “does not believe the paternal grandfather fully grasps the severity of the family violence to which she and the children had been exposed” (paragraph 27);

(g) At paragraphs 28 to 30, Mr H gives some context for the mother’s complaints about the father, before the mother is recorded as saying she “wanted the children to have a relationship with their father, but for them to be safe, protected, and for [the father] to get the help he needs in order to better manage and regulate his behaviour (paragraph 31);

(h) At paragraph 34, the mother shared her reflections and concerns about how best to progress forward, and that the father’s unpredictability causes her the greatest uncertainty. Mr H correctly and sensibly said (at paragraph 37) that “a testing of the evidence surrounding these allegations will obviously be of lynchpin significance”;

(i) The mother was “balanced, considered, and even reasonable when describing her concerns regarding [the father]” but emphasised that the father “needed to get help” (paragraph 39). At the time of the interviews, the mother’s belief was that time needs to be supervised, but not by the father’s family, who do not acknowledge the seriousness of his behaviour (paragraph 40);

(j) X presented well to Mr H who reported to the expert that:

(i) the fights between the parents had frightened him;

(ii) he expressed concern about his sister Y being scared if she witnessed the fight;

(iii) his mother is nice when not angry;

(iv) his father could get very angry;

(v) his comments about changes to his father:

I would suck him into a big machine, and I would have a nice and a not nice button .... I would press the completely nice button and he would come flying out and he be really really nice, and he would not get angry or mad at all .... he would let me do stuff and not be angry.
was revealing;

(vi) X was “nonetheless positive about his father”, but it was his mother whom he identified as has primary parent.

(k) Mr H opined that the nature of X’s spontaneous account is more typical of an accurate reflection by a child of X’s age (paragraph 43), and that although X loves his father, he “conveyed real ambivalence about him” however clearly wants to see his father and misses him (paragraph 45).

58 The conclusions of Mr H (paragraphs 46 to 57) read in their entirety support the opinion expressed at paragraph 56, which says:

Is difficult to make strong recommendations in the long-term given the untested nature of these proceedings. Nonetheless, given the age and stage of the children, for them to maintain a relationship with their father requires them to see him and regularly so; it may well be that shorter periods of time will place less pressure on an already strained situation, and without question, having others in attendance to provide assistance, support and reassurance would be by far the less uncertain alternative. Contact at least each alternate weekend on 1 or 2 days would be desirable, as would contact in the mid-week. It is difficult to recommend overnight or extended stays without some objective testing of the evidence. The allegations, the counter allegations, and even the self-portrayal of Mr [Rattan] of his overall functioning, and my observations of him suggest that a more conservative approach is indicated, but not to the point of complete exclusion. The emphasis must be on what can be done and what both parents can do in order to move the matter forward.
Under cross-examination, it was clear Mr H found an outcome, as sought by the mother, of no time, difficult to accept. Understandably, he, like the Court, sees a “no time” order as the worse option, as it robs the child of a sense of identity.
Mr H expressed (and supported it seems to me) concerns similar to those of Dr G about the prospect of any change to the father’s behaviour, and importantly insight, occurring without treatment – which the father has not undertaken consistently.
I accept that the general support by Mr H for some type of contact order was offered as a solution “with a tolerable and reasonable level of risk”, subject to findings on the hotly contested family violence issues, discussed above.

SECTION 60CC FACTORS

I rely upon but do not repeat findings already made above, as I now deal, in a narrative style, with the s 60CC factors. I also understand that s 60CG is highly relevant in this case, and in particular ensuring the parenting orders I make “does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence”.
As identified earlier when setting out the competing proposals of the parties, they substantially differ where:
(a) the mother contends for orders where the children “spend no time and have no communication with the father”;

(b) the father (by reference to Exhibit 1), seeks orders where the children’s time with him moves from supervised time, and subject to the father providing a hair follicle test showing no traces of illicit substances, to unsupervised time. The unsupervised time will (again subject to drug testing) graduate from seven hours to then overnight time (after 12 weeks). Eventually the father seeks (at order 9) every Wednesday overnight and each alternate weekend from after school Friday to before school Wednesday (essentially seven nights a fortnight). The father also seeks half of school holidays, and time on special days like Christmas, Easter and Father’s Day;

(c) the ICL’s proposed orders (as annexed) are founded on the father engaging with a psychologist and attending fortnightly for at least 12 months (order 6) and the parents engaging with a joint counsellor, as recommended by Mr H, for 12 months (order 7). On the basis this occurs, the children’s time with the father shall slowly progress by:

(i) for six months, one visit per month at a contact centre; then

(ii) each third Sunday of the month for seven hours with a paternal grandparent in “substantial attendance”;

(iii) after commencement of the daytime above, the children would also spend time on Father’s Day and the father’s birthday; and

(iv) order 15 requires the father to undertake hair follicle testing.

In essence, the ICL proposes long-term supervised time only.

It is not in dispute that the children will live with the mother and that she will exercise sole decision-making for major long-term decisions. I will now consider these competing proposals within the matrix of the factors identified in the statutory pathway.

What arrangements would promote the safety of the children and each person who has care of the children

My discussion above relating to family violence perpetrated by the father, means, in my assessment, the father poses a risk to the children and although I am not satisfied he has harmed X or Y physically, his lack of insight and future outcomes from his impulsive behaviour, is difficult to predict. It is a great pity the father did not seriously take up the concerns raised by both Dr G and Mr H, about the need for counselling. I have, at this time, little confidence he genuinely believes he needs such assistance.
In respect of drug testing, which has been a constant concern raised by the mother, the last test of the father was in June 2024, which revealed and detected cocaine use. The father gave little context as to why he was using cocaine, or what steps he is taking to stop using cocaine. The father did not undertake any further testing in the 10 months before the final hearing – claiming he could not afford to do so.
In my view, these risks that the father poses require at least supervised time restrictions, but whether it is possible to advance past supervised time is the real issue that I come to grips with below.
I do not find the mother poses any real risk to the safety of the children.

Views expressed

I have recorded what Mr H was able to obtain, by way of feelings, from X. At his age and level of maturity, his wishes are not determinative. Y was not, understandably, interviewed and has really had little time with the father since separation, when she was just a baby.

Developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the children

Mr H gave evidence of the effect on children who do not have regular contact with a parent. It can, I accept, adversely affect their sense of identity and a secure feeling of “who they are”. I take into account that X had a relationship with the father and missed seeing him. If the children spend time with the father that is supervised “long term”, because the father does not (for example) maintain his counselling and drug testing – the father moving in and out of their lives is, I assess, likely to have an adverse effect on their development and their psychological and emotional wellbeing.

Capacity to parent

The father’s capacity to parent is compromised by his lack of insight as to the effect the family violence he has perpetrated, has had on the mother and the children. His evidence showed he really has, in my view, fixed beliefs. It is mere speculation as to whether consistent therapy would have assisted. The mother’s history of the father’s abuse since 2014, which I have broadly accepted, reveals a long-standing issue the father has simply not dealt with. In some ways, the use of illicit substances is easier for the father to deal with – but the Court has no evidence to say he has done so. The lack of any evidence from the father’s previous counsellor Ms N was disappointing.
The mother’s capacity to parent, without having to engage with the father, has been consistently good. The evidence of her treating psychologist Ms M, who was not required for cross-examination, was founded on 25 counselling sessions since August 2023, and the report before the Court dated 17 March 2025, included this summary:
[Ms Saros] is committed to her psychological well-being and is actively engaging in therapy. She continues to experience distress, particularly related to concerns about safety, legal proceedings and uncertainty about the future. She attempts to apply the learned strategies; however, she is frequently left feeling overwhelmed. Continued work will focus on deepening trauma processing, strengthening self-worth, and fostering long-term emotional stability.
I accept this opinion from which I find the mother, unlike the father, both sees the benefit in psychological support and has committed to ongoing treatment. I find that even if the mother was confronted with orders that the children spend supervised time with the father, she would comply. However, Dr G opined it would likely have an adverse effect on her functioning, and as the unchallenged primary carer, it would likely have an adverse effect on her parenting – an outcome contrary to the children’s best interests. I accept that opinion.

The benefit to the children being able to have a relationship with the father and other people who are significant to the children

I have already identified the benefit to the children of having a relationship with the father. The father relied upon (although he had filed no case outline) affidavits from:
his father, Mr D Rattan;
his mother, Mrs E Rattan; and
his sister, Ms F.
They were not required for cross-examination. The evidence relating to parenting issues was clearly that they all felt they had a strong relationship with X; wish to develop a relationship with Y and were saddened (and somewhat confused) by the lack of opportunity they have had to spend time with the children. Really overall, there was nothing remarkable about their evidence, and I am content to find the children would benefit from having a relationship with the extended paternal family.
The mother’s concerns about the extended paternal family seem to stem from her belief that they do not accept the level and intensity of the family violence perpetrated upon her by the father since 2014. In circumstances where the mother herself acknowledged she had elected not to tell them about what was happening in the home until approaching separation, this criticism of the extended paternal family is unfair. With that history, it is unsurprising that they do not accept the mother’s version of events – particularly in the face of the consistent denials by the father.

Anything else that is relevant

The form of orders are stark in one aspect – the mother’s orders would (subject to a successful future application passing the threshold created by s 65DAAA) bring the litigation to an end – whereas each of the proposals of the father and ICL are conditional upon the father doing certain things in the future successfully. The likely success of the father doing something he has already known he should do (but has not done), weighs heavily in my mind. Prolonging this litigation especially where any result is likely to fuel the father’s sense of injustice and grievance, is an issue I need to consider.
I believe my earlier findings have dealt with s 60CC(2A), but in any event note the current Intervention Order (Exhibit 4), issued in 2025 by the Magistrates Court at Melbourne, was made with the consent of the father (without admission), and names the mother and children as “protected persons”. That Order expires in 2027 and contains the usual conditions.

CONCLUSION

This is the last parenting decision I have published before I retire after 25 years on the Bench. My reluctance to order “no time” for the children has generally made me feel uncomfortable – recognising the rights of children to spend time and have a relationship with their parents.
As Mr H rightly observed, absenting a parent by way of Court order, especially for younger children, is a “big call”. However, ongoing litigation has been proven, over the 50 years since the Act came into effect, to be one of the most harmful environments for children to navigate.
I am satisfied the currently untreated father (not receiving counselling and who also has not adequately dealt with an underlying illicit drug use history) does pose a risk to the children. To some extent, supervision might ameliorate the risks during a visit between the children and their father. Although, I do not fully share the mother’s concerns about the father’s extended family supervising visits – the concerns about whether supervised time can progress and in what manner still remain. The ICL does not propose moving past visits with grandparents being “substantially in attendance”.
Ms Metherell of Counsel, for the mother, urged the Court to make orders which are not overly aspirational, and which should remain within the realm of reality, on the evidence. After careful consideration, with a degree of regret, I have been persuaded by the submissions of Counsel for the mother, on all the evidence, that the orders generally sought by the mother are in the children’s best interests at this time.
In the exercise of my discretion, matters which shaped my conclusion that weigh in favour of the mother’s proposal include:
(a) the long history of family violence perpetrated by the father. Whilst it might be said there are far worse examples that present in matters before the Court (often accompanied by criminal convictions for assault, rape or child abuse), nonetheless the level and consistency of the violence in this matter, is highly relevant. I also accept that X, in particular, has observed and been affected by the father’s behaviour;

(b) the mother has a genuine fear of the father in my view;

(c) the father has minimised his contribution to the violent and aggressive events and shows, even now, little insight into its effect upon the mother and X;

(d) the father has failed, because in part of his lack of insight, to undertake and maintain therapeutic engagement – preferring, because it is easier – to believe his behaviour is entirely attributable to the diagnosis of ADD and other conditions, only recently made;

(e) the orders proposed by the father, apart from drug testing, identify no other conditions. His confidence that he can move so quickly to unsupervised time and then overnight time could work with X, but presents in my assessment, real difficulties for Y who does not currently have a relationship with the father. The form of orders, to some degree, demonstrate the father is more concerned about his rights as a father than understanding what is in the children’s best interests;

(f) the orders proposed by the ICL are highly conditional, and present a possibility of failure and then fresh legal proceedings for variation or enforcement likely to engulf the parents, including:

(i) ordering the father to attend a psychologist “approved by the ICL” does not guarantee a therapeutic relationship will develop – whatever skills the psychologist holds. If the power to make such an order exists (see Oberlin & Infeld [2021] FamCAFC 66) which confirms the power to order the father to undertake therapy can only be exercised as a condition of a parenting order made within power, where the father has demonstrated no appetite to receive help, is problematic;

(ii) the father gave evidence he was told he would get no benefit from a Men’s Behavioural Change Program the Court had ordered him to complete. I fear this father is likely to see attending upon a psychologist as merely “ticking a box” necessary to see his children;

(iii) empowering a yet unidentified psychologist to decide if the father’s treatment should extend for more than 12 months, can give no confidence to the mother that the father is “cured”. I hold real concerns about making such an order; and

(iv) long-term supervised time invites further litigation.

The matter, on the basis of the Orders I make which appear at the commencement of these Reasons, could return to the Court if the father brings a future application and navigates successfully the statutory requirements of s 65DAAA. The mother has revealed enough in her evidence to give me some level of comfort that if the father in the future can reveal he has taken his actions in perpetrating family violence seriously; has engaged consistently and genuinely in therapy and is drug free – then she will not necessarily oppose safe contact commencing. That is why I have decided against a “blunt” no time order but prefer to make an expansive order still, I accept, is unenforceable by the father really.
In many ways, the ball is in the father’s Court, but whether his clear love for his children and desire for him and his extended family to have a relationship with them, is enough to cause him to do something about his issues or not, only time will tell.
The Orders which appear at the commencement of these Reasons are in the children’s best interests on the evidence at this time.
